notes: Nigeria-focused AfroKitchen expansion wave covering northern, south-western, south-eastern, and south-south dishes. Recipes are synthesized from multiple Nigerian and diaspora references, not copied from a single source.

recipes.name: Tuwo Shinkafa
recipes.description: A northern Nigerian rice swallow cooked until soft, mashed smooth, and shaped into mounds for soups like miyan kuka or miyan taushe.
recipes.story: Tuwo Shinkafa is a northern Nigerian staple made from soft-cooked rice pounded or stirred into a smooth swallow. It is plain by design, because the soup beside it carries the seasoning.
recipes.occasion: Best for northern soup meals, family lunch, and any table built around a thick draw or leafy soup.
recipes.best_served_with: Miyan kuka, miyan taushe, okra soup, or groundnut soup.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use short-grain rice, some use broken rice, and some finish with rice flour if the pot needs more structure.
recipes.review_summary: References agree that tuwo shinkafa is rice cooked very soft, mashed, and shaped as a swallow. The AfroKitchen method uses broken or short-grain rice for a smooth finish.
recommended_changes: Use starchy short-grain or broken rice for the best texture.
Stir hard at the end so the rice becomes smooth and cohesive.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references and regional cooking guides.

steps.title: Cook the rice soft
steps.instruction: Add rice and water to a heavy pot. Bring to a boil, then cook until the grains are very soft.

steps.title: Mash the grains
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and mash with a wooden spoon or masher until the rice breaks down.

steps.title: Beat into tuwo
steps.instruction: Keep stirring and folding until the mixture pulls together into a smooth swallow.
steps.tip: Splash in hot water if it stiffens before becoming smooth.

steps.title: Shape and serve
steps.instruction: Scoop into wet bowls or wrap briefly to shape, then serve hot with soup.

recipes.name: Miyan Kuka
recipes.description: Northern Nigerian baobab leaf soup cooked with meat stock, dried fish, dawadawa, palm oil, chile, and fine kuka powder.
recipes.story: Miyan Kuka gets its unmistakable body from powdered baobab leaves. The soup is earthy, slightly tangy, and deeply northern Nigerian, especially when dawadawa and dried fish perfume the stock.
recipes.occasion: Best for tuwo meals, northern family lunch, and rainy-day soup bowls.
recipes.best_served_with: Tuwo shinkafa, tuwo masara, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use potash, some skip it, and meat can be beef, goat, dried fish, or a mix.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on kuka powder, meat stock, dawadawa, dried fish, palm oil, and pepper. The AfroKitchen version adds kuka off high heat so it does not clump.
recommended_changes: Sift the kuka powder before adding.
Sprinkle kuka gradually while stirring to avoid lumps.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: Yummy Medley - Miyan Kuka Baobab Leaves Soup

steps.title: Cook the meat
steps.instruction: Simmer meat with salt and water until tender and the stock tastes rich.

steps.title: Build the soup
steps.instruction: Add dried fish, dawadawa, pepper, crayfish if using, and palm oil. Simmer until the oil settles into the stock.

steps.title: Add kuka
steps.instruction: Lower the heat and sprinkle in sifted kuka powder while stirring constantly.
steps.tip: Do not dump the powder in one spot. It can clump fast.

steps.title: Finish gently
steps.instruction: Simmer briefly until the soup thickens and turns silky. Adjust salt and serve.

recipes.name: Miyan Taushe
recipes.description: Northern Nigerian pumpkin and groundnut soup with meat, leafy greens, tomato-pepper base, dawadawa, and warm spices.
recipes.story: Miyan Taushe is a rich northern Nigerian soup where pumpkin softens into the pot and groundnut paste gives body. It is generous, nutty, lightly sweet, and built for tuwo.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend northern meals, guests, and cold evenings.
recipes.best_served_with: Tuwo shinkafa, tuwo masara, rice, or couscous.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions include sorrel leaves, spinach, yakuwa, or bitter greens. The groundnut level changes by household.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on pumpkin, groundnut paste, meat or fish, tomato-pepper base, leafy greens, and northern seasonings like dawadawa.
recommended_changes: Mash part of the pumpkin into the soup and leave some pieces whole.
Thin groundnut paste before adding so it blends without scorching.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

steps.title: Cook meat and pumpkin
steps.instruction: Simmer meat with onion, salt, and water until nearly tender. Add pumpkin and cook until soft.

steps.title: Add tomato pepper base
steps.instruction: Stir in blended tomatoes and peppers, dawadawa, and dried pepper. Simmer until the raw tomato taste fades.

steps.title: Enrich with groundnut
steps.instruction: Loosen groundnut paste with warm stock, then stir it into the soup and simmer gently.
steps.tip: Stir often because groundnut can catch at the bottom.

steps.title: Finish with greens
steps.instruction: Mash some pumpkin into the soup, add greens, and cook until just tender. Adjust salt.

recipes.name: Masa
recipes.description: Northern Nigerian fermented rice cakes cooked in small wells until lightly crisp outside and soft inside.
recipes.story: Masa, also called waina, is a northern Nigerian rice cake with a gentle fermented tang. It is cooked in a special pan or an ebelskiver-style pan and eaten with pepper sauce, yaji, soup, or honey.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, market snacks, Ramadan tables, or a light supper.
recipes.best_served_with: Yaji, suya pepper, miyan taushe, honey, or spicy tomato sauce.
recipes.regional_variations: Some batters include cooked rice for softness, some use yeast, and some rely more on natural fermentation.
recipes.review_summary: References agree on soaked rice blended into a fermented batter and cooked in small oiled wells. The AfroKitchen version uses yeast for predictable home fermentation.
recommended_changes: Let the batter ferment until lightly bubbly.
Cook over medium heat so the centers set before the outside burns.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

steps.title: Soak the rice
steps.instruction: Soak raw rice overnight, then drain well.

steps.title: Blend the batter
steps.instruction: Blend soaked rice, cooked rice, sugar, salt, yeast, and warm water into a smooth thick batter.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover and leave the batter in a warm place until bubbly and slightly risen.

steps.title: Cook the masa
steps.instruction: Oil a masa pan, pour batter into each well, and cook until set and golden, turning once.

recipes.name: Dan Wake
recipes.description: Hausa bean flour dumplings boiled until tender, then dressed with oil, yaji, onions, tomato, and boiled egg.
recipes.story: Dan Wake is a northern Nigerian bean dumpling dish, humble and filling. The dumplings are mild, then the plate wakes up with yaji, oil, fresh onion, tomato, and egg.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, lunch, and quick street-food-style meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Yaji, pepper oil, sliced onions, tomatoes, cabbage, cucumber, and boiled eggs.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks add kuka powder to the dough, some use cassava flour as binder, and toppings vary from simple oil and pepper to a full salad plate.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on bean flour dumplings boiled in water and served with oil, pepper spice, onions, vegetables, and often boiled eggs.
recommended_changes: Make a soft dough that can be pinched or spooned into water.
Do not overcrowd the pot, so the dumplings do not stick together.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: Travel Food Atlas - Dan Wake Recipe

steps.title: Mix the dough
steps.instruction: Combine bean flour, cassava flour, optional kuka, salt, and enough water to make a soft sticky dough.

steps.title: Boil the dumplings
steps.instruction: Bring water to a rolling boil. Drop small spoonfuls of dough into the pot and cook until firm.

steps.title: Drain
steps.instruction: Lift the dumplings out with a slotted spoon and rinse briefly with hot water to separate them.

steps.title: Dress and serve
steps.instruction: Toss with oil and yaji, then top with onions, tomato, and boiled egg if using.

recipes.name: Amala, Ewedu and Gbegiri
recipes.description: A classic Yoruba plate of yam flour swallow served with jute leaf ewedu, silky bean gbegiri, and stew.
recipes.story: Amala with ewedu and gbegiri is one of the great Yoruba plates: dark yam flour swallow, stretchy jute leaf soup, bean soup, and red stew sharing one bowl.
recipes.occasion: Best for weekend lunch, buka-style plates, parties, and proper swallow cravings.
recipes.best_served_with: Assorted meat stew, ponmo, beef, goat, or fish.
recipes.regional_variations: Some plates use abula, the full combination of ewedu, gbegiri, and stew. Others serve only ewedu and stew.
recipes.review_summary: Sources support the three-part Yoruba combination: amala from yam flour, ewedu from jute leaves, and gbegiri from peeled beans, usually finished with stew.
recommended_changes: Cook the beans until very soft before blending for gbegiri.
Beat amala vigorously to remove lumps.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

ingredients.name: peeled brown beans or black-eyed peas

steps.title: Cook gbegiri
steps.instruction: Cook peeled beans until very soft, then blend smooth with cooking liquid.

steps.title: Finish gbegiri
steps.instruction: Return blended beans to the pot with palm oil, crayfish if using, and salt. Simmer until silky.

steps.title: Cook ewedu
steps.instruction: Boil ewedu leaves briefly with optional potash, then whisk or blend until stretchy. Season lightly.

steps.title: Make amala
steps.instruction: Bring water to a boil, reduce heat, sprinkle in yam flour, and beat until smooth and elastic.

steps.title: Plate abula style
steps.instruction: Serve amala with ewedu, gbegiri, and pepper stew in the same bowl.

recipes.name: Ayamase
recipes.description: Yoruba green pepper stew cooked with bleached palm oil, iru, assorted meats, and boiled eggs, usually served with Ofada rice.
recipes.story: Ayamase is intense, smoky, and unmistakably Yoruba. Green peppers, bleached palm oil, iru, and assorted meats create the stew that makes Ofada rice feel complete.
recipes.occasion: Best for parties, Sunday lunch, and serious rice plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Ofada rice, boiled eggs, fried plantain, or plain white rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use more green bell pepper, some more green scotch bonnet, and the meat mix depends on the table.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on green pepper blend, bleached palm oil, iru, assorted meats, and boiled eggs. The AfroKitchen method includes a safe cooling step for bleached palm oil.
recommended_changes: Bleach palm oil with ventilation and let it cool before opening the pot.
Reduce the pepper blend first so the stew fries instead of boiling.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: My Active Kitchen - Ofada Stew Ayamase

steps.title: Blend and reduce
steps.instruction: Blend green peppers and onion coarsely, then boil down until thick and most water evaporates.

steps.title: Bleach palm oil safely
steps.instruction: Heat palm oil in a covered pot over medium heat until darker and smoky, then turn off heat and let it cool before opening.
steps.tip: Ventilate the kitchen and never open the hot smoky pot toward your face.

steps.title: Fry the stew
steps.instruction: Add iru to the oil, then the reduced pepper blend. Fry until the oil begins to separate.

steps.title: Add meat and eggs
steps.instruction: Add assorted meats, crayfish if using, and salt. Simmer, then add eggs and coat well.

recipes.name: Boli
recipes.description: Nigerian roasted plantain served hot with groundnuts or a spicy pepper sauce.
recipes.story: Boli is the smell of Nigerian streets: plantain roasting over heat until charred, sweet, smoky, and soft. It can be as simple as groundnuts or as lively as pepper sauce.
recipes.occasion: Best for street-food plates, snacks, lunch, or barbecue sides.
recipes.best_served_with: Roasted groundnuts, pepper sauce, grilled fish, or avocado.
recipes.regional_variations: In some southern cities, boli is paired with fish and pepper sauce. In simpler versions, roasted groundnuts do all the work.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on ripe or semi-ripe plantain roasted until charred and tender, served with groundnuts or pepper sauce.
recommended_changes: Use ripe but firm plantains for a sweet, structured result.
Turn often so the plantain chars without burning through.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: All Nigerian Recipes - Boli Roasted Plantain

steps.title: Prepare plantains
steps.instruction: Peel plantains and leave them whole or cut each in half crosswise.

steps.title: Roast
steps.instruction: Roast over charcoal, grill, or hot oven, turning often until charred in spots and tender.

steps.title: Make pepper sauce
steps.instruction: Cook tomatoes, peppers, onion, oil, and salt until thick and glossy.

steps.title: Serve hot
steps.instruction: Serve roasted plantain with groundnuts, pepper sauce, or grilled fish.

recipes.name: Ofe Nsala
recipes.description: Igbo white soup made without palm oil, thickened with yam and cooked with catfish, crayfish, uziza, and utazi.
recipes.story: Ofe Nsala is called white soup because it skips palm oil. The flavor is clean and peppery, with yam thickener, catfish, crayfish, uziza, and a little utazi bitterness.
recipes.occasion: Best for swallow meals, special family cooking, and peppery fish soup cravings.
recipes.best_served_with: Pounded yam, semolina, fufu, or boiled yam.
recipes.regional_variations: Catfish is common, but chicken or goat can be used. Some cooks add more uziza for heat and aroma.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on no palm oil, yam thickener, catfish or meat, crayfish, pepper, uziza, and utazi. The AfroKitchen method keeps the fish pieces large so they do not break.
recommended_changes: Add fish after the stock is seasoned so it does not overcook.
Use pounded yam paste as the thickener, not flour slurry.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: Low Carb Africa - Ofe Nsala Nigerian White Soup

steps.title: Make yam thickener
steps.instruction: Boil yam until soft, then pound or blend into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Bring water or stock to a simmer with crayfish, uziza, pepper, and salt.

steps.title: Cook the fish
steps.instruction: Add catfish pieces and simmer gently until almost cooked.

steps.title: Thicken and finish
steps.instruction: Add yam paste in small lumps, simmer until dissolved and thickened, then finish with utazi.

recipes.name: Oha Soup
recipes.description: Igbo oha leaf soup cooked with cocoyam thickener, palm oil, meat, dried fish, crayfish, and ogiri.
recipes.story: Oha soup is one of the treasured Igbo soups, with tender oha leaves added late so their fragrance stays fresh. Cocoyam gives the soup body and palm oil gives it warmth.
recipes.occasion: Best for Sunday swallow, celebrations, and family cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Pounded yam, fufu, garri, or semolina.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks use achi or ofor as thickener when cocoyam is unavailable, but cocoyam is the classic route.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on oha leaves, cocoyam thickener, palm oil, meat, dried fish, crayfish, and ogiri. The AfroKitchen version tears the leaves by hand and adds them at the end.
recommended_changes: Do not overcook oha leaves.
Use cocoyam paste for the classic silkiness.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: My Diaspora Kitchen - Ora Oha Soup

steps.title: Cook meat and fish
steps.instruction: Simmer meat until tender, then add dried fish and continue until the stock is rich.

steps.title: Prepare cocoyam
steps.instruction: Boil cocoyam until soft, peel, and pound into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Thicken the soup
steps.instruction: Add palm oil, crayfish, pepper, ogiri if using, and cocoyam paste in small lumps. Simmer until thickened.

steps.title: Add oha leaves
steps.instruction: Stir in torn oha leaves and cook briefly until just wilted. Adjust salt.

recipes.name: Abacha
recipes.description: Igbo cassava salad tossed with palm oil dressing, ugba, fish, crayfish, onion, garden egg, and utazi.
recipes.story: Abacha is a bright Igbo cassava salad, chewy and savory with palm oil dressing, ugba, fish, crayfish, onion, garden egg, and bitter utazi accents.
recipes.occasion: Best for snacks, visitors, market-style meals, and festive side plates.
recipes.best_served_with: Garden eggs, fried fish, kpomo, onions, and chilled drinks.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions are fully mixed, others are plated with toppings. Ugba and utazi bring the unmistakable eastern Nigerian profile.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on shredded cassava, palm oil emulsion, potash water, ugba, fish, crayfish, onion, and utazi or garden egg toppings.
recommended_changes: Rinse softened abacha well so it is tender but not soggy.
Use potash water sparingly to emulsify palm oil.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: All Nigerian Recipes - African Salad Abacha
My Diaspora Kitchen - African Salad Abacha

steps.title: Soften abacha
steps.instruction: Soak abacha in warm water until flexible, then rinse and drain well.

steps.title: Make palm oil dressing
steps.instruction: Stir palm oil with strained potash water until it thickens and turns bright orange.

steps.title: Season the salad
steps.instruction: Add crayfish, pepper, salt, ugba, and fish to the dressing, then toss in abacha.

steps.title: Finish with toppings
steps.instruction: Top with onion, garden egg, and utazi. Serve warm or room temperature.

recipes.name: Okpa
recipes.description: Eastern Nigerian Bambara nut pudding steamed with palm oil, pepper, onion, and salt until firm and tender.
recipes.story: Okpa is a beloved eastern Nigerian breakfast and road-trip food made from Bambara nut flour. The batter is simple, but the steamed result is rich, nutty, and filling.
recipes.occasion: Best for breakfast, travel food, school lunch, and make-ahead snacks.
recipes.best_served_with: Pap, custard, tea, or eaten on its own.
recipes.regional_variations: Some cooks steam okpa in banana leaves, some in cups or bags. Enugu-style okpa is especially famous.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on Bambara nut flour, palm oil, pepper, salt, and steaming in wraps or containers. The AfroKitchen version adds onion as optional because some cooks skip it.
recommended_changes: Use warm water to hydrate the flour smoothly.
Steam fully so the center sets and loses raw bean taste.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

steps.title: Mix flour and oil
steps.instruction: Rub palm oil into okpa flour until evenly colored.

steps.title: Make batter
steps.instruction: Add warm water gradually with pepper, onion if using, and salt. Mix into a pourable batter.

steps.title: Fill wraps
steps.instruction: Pour batter into banana leaves, heatproof cups, or food-safe steaming bags, leaving room to expand.

steps.title: Steam until set
steps.instruction: Steam until firm all the way through and no raw bean smell remains.

recipes.name: Afang Soup
recipes.description: Efik-Ibibio vegetable soup with shredded afang leaves, waterleaf, palm oil, meat, stockfish, crayfish, and periwinkles.
recipes.story: Afang soup is a South-South Nigerian classic, dense with greens and rich with seafood notes. Afang leaves bring chew and depth while waterleaf keeps the pot lush.
recipes.occasion: Best for swallow meals, celebrations, and Sunday cooking.
recipes.best_served_with: Garri, fufu, pounded yam, or semolina.
recipes.regional_variations: Periwinkles, stockfish, smoked fish, beef, and kpomo all vary by household, but afang and waterleaf remain the backbone.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on afang or okazi leaves, waterleaf, palm oil, meat, stockfish, crayfish, and often periwinkles. The AfroKitchen version cooks waterleaf first so it releases moisture before afang is added.
recommended_changes: Slice or pound afang leaves finely so they soften.
Do not add too much water. The greens should carry the soup.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

steps.title: Cook the protein
steps.instruction: Simmer meat and stockfish until tender and the stock is reduced.

steps.title: Add waterleaf
steps.instruction: Add waterleaf and cook until it wilts and releases moisture.

steps.title: Season richly
steps.instruction: Add palm oil, crayfish, pepper, periwinkles if using, and salt. Simmer until glossy.

steps.title: Finish with afang
steps.instruction: Stir in afang leaves and cook until softened but still green.

recipes.name: Edikang Ikong
recipes.description: Efik-Ibibio vegetable soup loaded with ugu, waterleaf, palm oil, meat, stockfish, crayfish, and periwinkles.
recipes.story: Edikang Ikong is a vegetable-rich South-South soup where the greens are the main event. Ugu and waterleaf cook down with seafood, meat, palm oil, and crayfish into a dense, celebratory pot.
recipes.occasion: Best for celebrations, Sunday lunch, and hearty swallow meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Garri, fufu, pounded yam, or semolina.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use spinach outside Nigeria, but fluted pumpkin leaves and waterleaf are the classic combination.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on ugu, waterleaf, palm oil, assorted meat, stockfish, crayfish, and periwinkle. The AfroKitchen method keeps added water low so the soup stays concentrated.
recommended_changes: Let waterleaf release moisture before adding ugu.
Keep the pot dense rather than watery.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: My Diaspora Kitchen - Edikang Ikong Soup

steps.title: Cook the protein
steps.instruction: Cook assorted meat and stockfish until tender with a small amount of stock left.

steps.title: Add waterleaf
steps.instruction: Stir in waterleaf and cook until it wilts and releases liquid.

steps.title: Season the soup
steps.instruction: Add palm oil, crayfish, pepper, periwinkles if using, and salt. Simmer until the oil blends in.

steps.title: Finish with ugu
steps.instruction: Add ugu leaves and cook briefly until tender and bright. Serve hot.

recipes.name: Banga Soup
recipes.description: Nigerian palm nut soup with meat or fish, banga spices, dried fish, crayfish, chile, and fresh herbs.
recipes.story: Banga soup is a southern Nigerian palm nut soup, especially loved in the Niger Delta. The palm fruit extract gives body, while banga spices, dried fish, crayfish, and herbs bring its signature aroma.
recipes.occasion: Best for starch, eba, fufu, rice, and weekend family meals.
recipes.best_served_with: Starch, eba, pounded yam, white rice, or fufu.
recipes.regional_variations: Delta-style banga often uses banga spice blends and starch, while Igbo ofe akwu leans toward rice and a slightly different herb profile.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on palm nut extract, meat or fish, dried fish, crayfish, pepper, and regional banga spices. The AfroKitchen version uses canned palm nut concentrate as an accessible option.
recommended_changes: Simmer long enough for the palm nut soup to thicken naturally.
Add delicate herbs late so their aroma stays fresh.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: My Diaspora Kitchen - Nigerian Banga Soup

steps.title: Cook protein
steps.instruction: Cook meat or fish with salt until tender, keeping the stock.

steps.title: Add palm base
steps.instruction: Stir palm nut concentrate into stock with water and simmer until it loosens and starts to thicken.

steps.title: Season deeply
steps.instruction: Add dried fish, crayfish, banga spice, pepper, and salt. Simmer until the soup is glossy.

steps.title: Finish with herbs
steps.instruction: Add scent leaf or bitterleaf and simmer briefly before serving.

recipes.name: Fisherman Soup
recipes.description: South-South Nigerian seafood soup with fresh fish, prawns, periwinkles, crayfish, pepper, yam thickener, and aromatic leaves.
recipes.ethnic_group: Rivers, Akwa Ibom, and coastal Nigerian
recipes.story: Fisherman soup is coastal Nigerian comfort, built around fresh seafood and a spicy broth. Fish, prawns, periwinkles, crayfish, and aromatic leaves make it taste like the river and the kitchen met in one pot.
recipes.occasion: Best for seafood lovers, Sunday lunch, and swallow meals by the tableful.
recipes.best_served_with: Fufu, eba, pounded yam, boiled plantain, or rice.
recipes.regional_variations: Some versions use yam or cocoyam as thickener, some add okra, and seafood changes with what is fresh.
recipes.review_summary: Sources agree on mixed seafood, fresh fish, prawns, periwinkles or shellfish, crayfish, pepper, and a light thickener. The AfroKitchen method adds delicate seafood late to avoid overcooking.
recommended_changes: Use firm fish so the pieces hold together.
Add prawns near the end so they stay tender.
recipes.official_source_status: No direct Nigerian ministry recipe page was found in this batch pass. The recipe is based on Nigerian culinary references.

sources.title: My Diaspora Kitchen - Nigerian Fisherman Soup

steps.title: Make thickener
steps.instruction: Boil yam or cocoyam until soft, then pound into a smooth paste.

steps.title: Season the broth
steps.instruction: Simmer stock with onion, crayfish, pepper, and salt until flavorful.

steps.title: Cook the fish
steps.instruction: Add fish steaks and simmer gently until almost cooked.

steps.title: Finish seafood
steps.instruction: Add prawns, periwinkles if using, thickener, and herbs. Simmer until the soup thickens and seafood is just cooked.
